doc_type: California DMV DS-367 Age 21 and Older Officer's Statement / Notice of Administrative Per Se Suspension and Temporary Driver License — Chemical Test Refusal (CVC §§13353, 13353.1, 13353.2 & 13389)

arguments: The officer lacked probable cause to arrest under CVC §40300.5: The driver was found asleep in a vehicle parked on the freeway shoulder, not observed driving. Arrest without a warrant under §40300.5 requires an independent basis (e.g., reasonable cause to believe the person drove while impaired). The officer's reasonable cause rested solely on the admission of driving (which the driver gave without Miranda warnings), the orange wristband, odor of alcohol, and objective symptoms — all post-driving observations.

The admission of driving is the linchpin of the arrest and may be challengeable: Because no officer observed the driving, the prosecution and DMV must rely on the driver's admission. The circumstances under which the admission was obtained (driver woken from sleep, confused and disoriented, interrogated without advisement) may raise issues of voluntariness or coercion.

Chemical test refusal vs. blood test collected: The form checks 'Chemical Test Refusal' as the violation, yet a blood test was collected at 07:38 AM on 11/01/2024 — approximately 6 hours and 26 minutes after arrest. The coexistence of a 'refusal' designation and a subsequently collected blood sample raises the question of whether a true, unequivocal refusal occurred or whether the driver ultimately submitted. If the driver submitted to blood testing, the refusal-based suspension may be improper.

Blood test collected nearly 6.5 hours after arrest at 07:38 AM: Under California law and Title 17 CCR, blood draw timing and chain of custody are critical. A sample drawn 6.5 hours after the time of driving may not accurately reflect BAC at the time of driving, and results should be evaluated with retrograde extrapolation analysis.

Absence of PAS test data: The PAS section is entirely blank. If a PAS test was offered and refused, or if it was not offered at all, the record is incomplete. The failure to administer or record a PAS test may be relevant to the completeness of the chemical test admonition process.

Driving not observed — CVC §40300.5 arrest prerequisites: Because neither the arresting officer nor Officer Melano observed driving, the arrest was warrantless under §40300.5. The DMV hearing will require the officer to establish each §40300.5 predicate: (1) the person was involved in a traffic accident, or (2) the person was observed in or about a vehicle blocking traffic, or (3) the person was found in or about a vehicle located in a roadway. Being parked on the shoulder of I-580 may or may not satisfy the 'blocking traffic' or 'in or about a roadway' prongs depending on the precise location.

Driver's statement that he had been parked approximately 7 minutes cuts against an inference of recent impaired driving: A 7-minute delay between stopping driving and officer contact, combined with no observations of the vehicle in motion, makes it difficult to establish contemporaneous impairment at the time of actual driving.

Driver explicitly denied alcohol consumption: The narrative records that when asked about alcohol consumption, the driver responded 'no.' This denial, if not impeached by the blood test results, supports a defense of no knowing or willful impaired driving.

Orange '21+' wristband as pretext or inference: The officer's specific notation of the wristband suggests it was used to infer the driver had been at an event serving alcohol. This is circumstantial at best and does not establish actual impairment.

Officer's printed name and badge number discrepancy between pages: Page 1 lists Officer MEZA, Badge 23295; Page 3 (Suspension Order) repeats this. However, the telephone number area code listed is (915) — a Texas area code — which may be a transcription error for (916) or another California prefix, and may indicate a clerical error on the form.

Field Sobriety Tests referenced but not detailed: The narrative references a 'DUI evaluation' was conducted and 'pulse FSTs' are noted under Objective Symptoms, but no standardized field sobriety test (SFST) results (HGN, Walk and Turn, One Leg Stand) are documented on the form. Absence of documented FST results weakens the probable cause basis if challenged.

The suspension is administrative and independent of any criminal court sanction: The APS suspension proceeds on a preponderance standard at the DMV, but any result does not bind or preclude the criminal case, and vice versa.

Other documents in matter-07 folder potentially cross-referencing this arrest: Arrest Report F7953179, DAD 2024-12-04, DS2044 Notification of Discovery, FAC Newanforbi v Candelaria, F7953179 Discovery Response, Newanforbi v. Candelaria
procedural_significance: This DS-367 is the foundational document initiating the administrative per se (APS) suspension of Brendan Newanforbi's California driving privilege. The form simultaneously serves as (1) the officer's sworn statement supporting probable cause for the arrest, (2) the notice of suspension/revocation order, and (3) the temporary driver license valid for 30 days. The critical procedural tension in this document is the designation of 'Chemical Test Refusal' as the grounds for suspension, while the form also reflects that a blood draw was in fact collected at 07:38 AM on 11/01/2024 — approximately 6.5 hours post-arrest. If the blood sample was collected, a question arises whether the refusal was unequivocal, whether the driver ultimately consented, or whether the blood was drawn involuntarily. The resolution of this issue determines whether the suspension is properly grounded in refusal (CVC §13353 — 1-year first-offense suspension) or should have been based on BAC results (CVC §13353.2 — 4-month first-offense suspension), which are meaningfully different in duration and legal theory. The arrest itself is warrantless and made under CVC §40300.5 because no officer observed the driving. This means the APS hearing will turn on whether the officer had reasonable cause to believe the driver drove while impaired and whether the §40300.5 statutory prerequisites are satisfied. The driver's spontaneous admission to having driven from a party in Oakland is the primary evidence of actual driving, and the circumstances of that admission — woken from sleep, confused, not yet under Miranda — may be challenged in the parallel criminal proceeding, though Miranda does not directly apply to the APS administrative hearing. The 10-day deadline to request a DMV hearing from the date of receipt of the notice is the immediate procedural action item. Failure to timely request a hearing results in the suspension becoming effective 30 days after issuance without any administrative review. The existence of a civil complaint captioned 'Newanforbi v. Candelaria' in the matter folder suggests this matter has expanded beyond the DUI arrest into civil litigation, possibly a civil rights or excessive force claim, which would make the DS-367's contents relevant not only to the DMV and criminal proceedings but also as evidence in the civil action regarding the lawfulness of the stop and arrest.
